planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Angelonia is one of the most genuinely heat-tolerant flowering annuals for zone 9 — actively thriving in the peak summer heat (June–September) that causes many annuals to stop blooming, become diseased, or die. The Serena series is one of the most widely grown and commercially successful compact angelonia series — bred for uniform habit, early flowering, and deep purple color intensity. Plants bloom continuously from planting through frost without deadheading. The foliage has a pleasant grape-like fragrance when brushed. Angelonia is increasingly used as a substitute for snapdragon in heat-tolerant summer situations — it provides a similar spike-form flower in conditions that kill snapdragons.

directions: Plant in full sun — angelonia requires full sun for maximum flowering and compact growth; partial shade causes leggy, reduced bloom — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil; Serena Purple Angelonia is a compact cultivar of Angelonia (Angelonia angustifolia), a tropical perennial from Mexico grown as a warm-season annual, producing upright spikes of small, two-lipped, orchid-like deep purple flowers continuously throughout the summer growing season in zone 9 without deadheading or special care. The unusual flower form — small, orchid-like flowers with two prominent lobes arranged alternately along the spike — is entirely different from most annual flowers, providing a distinctive floral texture that works well in mixed container and border compositions. Angelonia angustifolia is one of the best-adapted flowering annuals for the zone 9 climate: actively growing and blooming through the hottest, most humid part of zone 9 summer (July–September) when temperatures regularly exceed 95°F and humidity remains high — conditions that stress and reduce flowering in most other summer annuals. The combination of unmatched heat tolerance, continuous non-stop bloom without deadheading, attractive spike-form flowers, and pleasant fragrance makes Serena Purple one of the most practically valuable purple-flowering annuals for zone 9 summer beds and containers.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Angelonia angustifolia for Florida Summer Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Summer Annuals for Zone 9
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Flowers for Gulf South Summer Performance

planting.note: Plant transplants in spring as soon as last hard freeze danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–March for spring bloom. Superbells Yellow is part of the Superbells series from Proven Winners — one of the major Calibrachoa breeding programs. Calibrachoa requires excellent drainage and acidic conditions (pH 5.5–6.0); alkaline soils cause iron deficiency (interveinal chlorosis) corrected with chelated iron. Feed regularly with an acid-forming fertilizer as heavy feeder. In zone 9 calibrachoa performs best February–May and September–November; summer heat causes decline. It is not grown from seed — all Superbells calibrachoa are vegetatively propagated to maintain the specific cultivar. The trailing habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets and container edges.

directions: Plant in full sun — calibrachoa requires full sun for maximum flowering; partial shade reduces bloom significantly — in well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.0), fertile potting mix or garden soil; feed regularly with acid-forming fertilizer; Superbells Yellow Calibrachoa is a trailing, small-flowered annual producing hundreds of small (1-inch), bright yellow, petunia-like flowers on trailing stems that cascade elegantly over container edges — earning the common name 'Million Bells' for the sheer quantity of flowers produced per plant throughout the cool-season bloom period. Calibrachoa (Calibrachoa x hybrida) is a relatively recent container annual that emerged commercially in the 1990s and rapidly became one of the most important container and hanging basket plants in the North American market — providing the prolific, self-cleaning, non-deadheading flower production of wave petunias at a smaller, finer scale. The self-cleaning habit — spent flowers fall from the plant without leaving behind brown, dried remains that require removal — makes Superbells Yellow a maintenance-free container plant that requires only regular watering and fertilization to maintain its bloom display from spring through early summer in zone 9. The bright, clean yellow color provides a warm, sunny complement in mixed container compositions with blue, orange, and white companion plants.
sources: Proven Winners — Superbells Yellow Calibrachoa Grower Information
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calibrachoa for Florida Container Gardens
North Carolina State University Extension — Calibrachoa Cultivar Performance Trials

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Surdiva Blue is a selection from the Surdiva series bred specifically for heat and humidity tolerance — making it more reliable than older scaevola selections in Gulf South summer conditions. The trailing, spreading habit makes scaevola ideal for hanging baskets, container edges, and window boxes where the long, cascading stems create a flowing effect. Scaevola is propagated vegetatively from cuttings — not available as seed. It performs well in both full sun and partial shade in zone 9. The unique, fan-shaped flower form — five petals all arranged on one side of the flower, leaving the other side open — is botanically unusual and immediately recognizable.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil — Surdiva Blue Scaevola performs well in both sun and partial shade, making it one of the more flexible summer annuals for zone 9 mixed container planting; Surdiva Blue Scaevola is a trailing annual from Australia producing unusual, fan-shaped, lavender-blue to blue-violet flowers in which all five petals are arranged to one side of the bloom — a unique floral structure called a zygomorphic or bilateral flower that gives scaevola an immediately recognizable, unusual appearance among commonly grown annuals. The common name 'Fan Flower' describes this distinctive one-sided petal arrangement that distinguishes scaevola from every other commonly grown annual. The trailing stems (extending 18–30 inches from the container center) covered with the lavender-blue fan flowers create an airy, flowing cascade that is particularly effective in hanging baskets and elevated containers where the trailing stems are visible from below. In zone 9's warm, humid summer climate, scaevola maintains active growth and bloom through the peak heat that reduces flowering in many spring annuals — the native Australian origin giving it natural adaptability to warm, dry to moderately moist conditions similar to zone 9's summer.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Scaevola aemula for Florida Containers
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Australian Summer Annuals for Zone 9
Suntory Flowers — Surdiva Series Scaevola Production Guide

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Sunstorm Pink is a selection in the Sunstorm series of vinca bred for enhanced heat tolerance and mildew resistance. Annual vinca (Catharanthus roseus) is susceptible to aerial Phytophthora blight in conditions of poor drainage and water on the foliage — plant in raised, well-drained sites and avoid overhead irrigation or overhead watering. In zone 9 annual vinca is one of the most reliable and self-cleaning summer annuals available — requiring no deadheading, maintaining compact form without pinching, and blooming continuously from late spring through frost. Seeds require light for germination — do not cover; bottom heat improves germination speed.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil — Annual Vinca performs best in heat and full sun, declining in shade and in wet, poorly drained conditions; Sunstorm Pink Vinca is a compact selection of Annual Vinca (Catharanthus roseus), a heat-tolerant tropical annual from Madagascar producing a continuous cascade of five-petaled, flat-faced, bright pink flowers (each with a lighter central eye) that are self-cleaning, requiring no deadheading, and bloom from first warm weather through frost. The Sunstorm series was developed specifically for improved Phytophthora blight resistance and superior heat performance — addressing the primary weakness of older vinca series in the humid Gulf South climate where standing water or overhead irrigation created disease pressure on less resistant selections. Annual vinca is among the most genuinely heat-tolerant and low-water-demand flowering annuals available for zone 9 — thriving in the peak summer conditions of July–September when temperatures exceed 95°F and rainfall may be irregular. The combination of continuous self-cleaning bloom without deadheading, excellent drought tolerance once established, compact mounding habit, and genuine heat performance that improves through the hottest months of zone 9 summer makes Sunstorm Pink one of the most practically reliable summer flowering annuals for full-sun beds, borders, and containers.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vinca (Catharanthus roseus) for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Summer Annuals for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Vinca Series Comparison for Zone 9

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 sow March–May. Million Gold is a compact, very early-flowering melampodium selection that begins blooming in just 50–70 days from seed — among the earliest-blooming selections in this genus. The plant is extremely self-sufficient once established — no deadheading, no pinching, no staking, and minimal water requirements after root establishment. In zone 9 melampodium performs best in the heat of summer (May–October), with declining performance as fall temperatures cool. The bright golden-yellow flowers complement blue, purple, and white companions effectively; excellent with angelonia, vinca, and ornamental sweet potato. Plants can be started from direct sow seed — unusually easy for a warm-season annual.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Million Gold Melampodium thrives in hot, dry conditions and performs most reliably in full sun with minimal water once established; Million Gold Melampodium is a compact selection of Medallion Flower (Melampodium divaricatum), a warm-season annual from Mexico producing a neat, mounding, self-branching plant covered with small (1–1.5 inch), bright golden-yellow, single daisy flowers with deep yellow centers that bloom continuously from late spring through fall frost without any deadheading or flower removal. Melampodium is one of the most genuinely self-sufficient summer annuals available for zone 9 — requiring no deadheading (spent flowers drop cleanly from the plant), minimal water once established, no pinching or shaping, and performing actively through the peak zone 9 summer heat without the bloom reduction or disease issues that affect less heat-adapted annuals. The consistent, clean golden-yellow of the flowers — produced in sufficient quantity to give the plant a solid golden appearance — combined with the dwarf, non-flopping habit and absolute minimal-care requirements makes Million Gold one of the most practical choices for no-maintenance summer color in full-sun beds, borders, and containers throughout zone 9's long summer season.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Melampodium for Florida Annual Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Low-Maintenance Summer Annuals for Zone 9
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Melampodium for Gulf South Summer Color
